content.text: When a language model reasons incorrectly on a single pass, the error is silent — the model returns a confident-sounding answer with no indication of doubt. Self-consistency addresses this by sampling

from the same prompt at non-zero temperature, then selecting the answer that appears most often across those chains. The result is a calibrated, ensemble-like technique that substantially improves accuracy on tasks that have a deterministic correct answer — without any additional training.

Self-consistency is a decoding strategy for chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting introduced by Wang et al. (2023). Rather than committing to the single greedy output (temperature = 0), the technique samples

completions at a positive temperature from the same CoT prompt. Each completion produces an intermediate reasoning trace and a final answer. The answers are extracted and aggregated by majority vote; the most common answer is returned as the final result.
The key insight is that robust answers tend to be reachable via many different reasoning paths, whereas incorrect answers tend to arise from a narrow set of coincidental errors. When the model takes

independent paths, correct answers accumulate votes while incorrect answers cancel out. This is structurally similar to ensemble methods in classical machine learning — bagging, random forests — but applied at inference time over reasoning trajectories rather than model parameters.

Self-consistency requires no fine-tuning, no specialized model architecture, and no external verifier. It is a pure prompt-and-sampling technique, making it applicable to any language model API that exposes a temperature parameter. The technique pairs naturally with chain-of-thought prompting but also generalises to other reasoning formats, including programs (program-of-thought) and structured scratchpads.

Single-pass CoT prompting improves accuracy over direct-answer prompting, but it still fails in a predictable pattern: when the model takes one wrong step early in the reasoning chain, every subsequent step compounds the error, and the model arrives at a wrong answer with high surface confidence. There is no built-in signal that the output is unreliable.
Self-consistency exposes this hidden variance. If five out of seven samples agree on an answer, that agreement is a

— the model is likely correct. If samples split two-way or three-way, the low margin is an explicit signal of uncertainty. Orchestrators can act on this signal: retry with more samples, route to a more powerful model, flag for human review, or refuse to commit to a tool call that is under-specified.

In agentic systems, where model outputs drive irreversible actions — sending emails, executing database writes, making API calls — the cost of a single silent wrong answer can be high. Self-consistency shifts the failure mode from silent errors to detectable disagreement, which is far easier to handle in a robust pipeline.

Empirically, self-consistency delivers substantial gains: the original paper reported roughly 17 percentage-point improvement on GSM8K math benchmarks and 11 points on SVAMP over standard CoT with just 40 samples. Even modest sample counts (N = 5 to 10) recover the majority of the benefit at a fraction of the cost.
majority voting smooths out one-off errors in individual reasoning chains, consistently outperforming single-pass CoT on arithmetic, symbolic, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks.
the vote margin (fraction of samples agreeing) is a calibrated proxy for answer confidence, enabling downstream routing decisions without a separate confidence model.
the technique works at inference time with any model that accepts a temperature parameter — it requires no RLHF, no reward model, and no labelled data.
self-consistency stacks with chain-of-thought, program-of-thought, few-shot prompting, and role-based system prompts; any of these can be the underlying single-sample strategy being aggregated.
the N samples are independent and can be dispatched concurrently, so wall-clock latency scales with a single completion rather than N completions when compute is available.
on easy tasks where every sample agrees, self-consistency adds near-zero overhead while preserving full coverage for hard tasks where it matters.
Token cost scales linearly with N:
sampling 10 completions costs 10× the tokens of a single completion. For large context windows or long reasoning traces, this can become expensive quickly.
Not suited to open-ended or subjective tasks:
majority vote over free-form text generation is ill-defined. The technique requires tasks with a discrete, extractable answer — a number, a label, a boolean, or a short canonical string.

if the model does not produce its final answer in a consistent, parsable format, normalising and aggregating answers becomes error-prone. "42", "The answer is 42", and "42.0" must all map to the same answer.

when the model consistently misunderstands the question — a systematic bias rather than random variance — all N samples may agree on the same wrong answer. Majority vote cannot fix a shared blind spot.
most of the accuracy gain is captured by N = 10 to 20 samples; going to N = 40 provides marginal additional benefit and costs 2–4× more.
the caller must implement parallel dispatch, response collection, answer normalisation, vote counting, and confidence thresholding — none of which a basic LLM API call provides out of the box.
The algorithm has three stages: sampling, extraction, and aggregation.
The prompt includes chain-of-thought instructions ("Think step by step", or few-shot exemplars with worked solutions). The same prompt is submitted to the model N times with temperature

. Each call produces an independent completion whose reasoning trace is different, because the non-zero temperature introduces stochastic variation at every token decision. Typical values are T = 0.5 to T = 0.9; very low temperatures reduce diversity, and very high temperatures introduce too much noise.
The sampling distribution over answers is approximately:
is the selected answer, and count(a) is the number of samples that produced answer a.
Each completion must be parsed to extract a final answer. Extraction strategies include:
instruct the model to always end its reasoning with a delimiter such as "Final answer:" and extract everything after that delimiter.
for numeric answers, a pattern such as
captures the last number in the completion.
use JSON mode or a grammar-constrained decoder to force the model to emit the answer in a machine-readable field, eliminating parsing ambiguity entirely.
canonicalise extracted answers before counting — strip whitespace, lowercase, normalise numeric formats, strip units where the answer is a pure number.
Count occurrences of each unique normalised answer across the N samples and return the most frequent one. The confidence estimate is the vote margin:

A threshold on this confidence value controls routing: if confidence falls below, for example, 0.6 (fewer than 60 % of samples agree), the result is flagged as uncertain and routed to a fallback strategy — more samples, a stronger model, or a human escalation path.

Weighted variants of majority vote exist: rather than counting samples equally, each sample's vote is weighted by the model's token-level log-probabilities for the answer tokens. This generally outperforms unweighted voting but requires access to the model's logprobs, which not all APIs expose.

The diagram below illustrates the full self-consistency pipeline. A single prompt fans out to N parallel LLM calls, each producing an independent reasoning trace and final answer. The aggregator counts answers and returns the majority winner along with a confidence score. Low confidence triggers a configurable escalation path.

attrs.alt: Self-Consistency architecture: prompt fans out to N parallel LLM samples, answers are aggregated by majority vote

content.text: The fan-out and fan-in are typically implemented by the orchestrator layer — a Python script, a LangChain chain, or an agent framework. The LLM API itself is stateless; each sample is an independent API call sharing only the prompt.
The architecture separates concerns cleanly: the
defines what reasoning style to use; the
normalises raw text into discrete answers; and the
implements the voting policy. Each layer can be swapped independently — for example, replacing simple majority vote with a weighted vote using logprobs touches only the aggregation layer.
Mathematical reasoning — arithmetic, algebra, word problems — where a single numeric answer is correct.
Symbolic logic, constraint satisfaction, and planning problems that have a verifiable solution.
Multi-hop question answering where the correct entity can be extracted and normalised.
Classification tasks where the label space is small and discrete.
Code generation where the correct function name or return value can be compared.
Agent decision nodes where a wrong tool call or parameter value has downstream cost.
Open-ended summarisation, creative writing, or explanation generation — there is no single correct answer to vote on.
Tasks where the model has a systematic bias that affects all samples equally; more samples do not help.
High-throughput, latency-sensitive pipelines where N× cost cannot be absorbed.
Tasks where the answer cannot be reliably extracted and normalised from free-form text.

Run a small offline eval: sample N = 5 completions on a held-out set and measure whether majority vote improves accuracy over single-pass greedy. If the improvement is less than 2 percentage points, self-consistency is not adding value for that task and should be removed from the pipeline.

A production-ready self-consistency implementation requires four components: a parallel sampler, an answer extractor, a normaliser, and a vote aggregator. The steps below build each one in Python using the Anthropic SDK.
that ends with a consistent delimiter such as "Final answer: <value>". Instruct the model to always end its response with this line.
or a thread pool. Collect all completions.
from each completion by splitting on the delimiter or applying a regex.
strip whitespace, lowercase, and convert numeric strings to a canonical format.
and return the most common answer with its confidence.
if confidence < threshold, raise an exception or return a sentinel value to trigger escalation in the caller.

# self_consistency.py — production implementation
import asyncio
import re
from collections import Counter
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

client = AsyncAnthropic()

COT_PROMPT = """Solve the following problem step by step. Show your work clearly.
At the very end, write:
Final answer: <your answer here>

Problem: {problem}
"""


def extract_answer(completion: str) -> str | None:
    """Parse the text after 'Final answer:' from a completion."""
    match = re.search(r"Final answer:\s*(.+)", completion, re.IGNORECASE)
    if match:
        return match.group(1).strip()
    return None


def normalise(answer: str) -> str:
    """Canonicalise an answer for comparison."""
    answer = answer.lower().strip()
    # Remove trailing punctuation
    answer = answer.rstrip(".")
    # Normalise numeric values: '42.0' -> '42', '042' -> '42'
    try:
        answer = str(int(float(answer)))
    except ValueError:
        pass
    return answer


async def sample_once(problem: str, temperature: float) -> str | None:
    response = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        temperature=temperature,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": COT_PROMPT.format(problem=problem)}],
    )
    text = response.content[0].text
    return extract_answer(text)


async def self_consistent_answer(
    problem: str,
    n: int = 7,
    temperature: float = 0.7,
    confidence_threshold: float = 0.0,
) -> dict:
    """
    Returns:
        {
          'answer': str,          # majority-vote winner
          'confidence': float,    # fraction of samples that agree
          'vote_counts': dict,    # full distribution
          'n_valid': int,         # samples with a parseable answer
        }
    Raises ValueError if confidence < confidence_threshold.
    """
    tasks = [sample_once(problem, temperature) for _ in range(n)]
    raw_answers = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    valid = [normalise(a) for a in raw_answers if a is not None]
    if not valid:
        raise ValueError("No samples produced a parseable answer")

    counts = Counter(valid)
    winner, top_count = counts.most_common(1)[0]
    confidence = top_count / len(valid)

    if confidence < confidence_threshold:
        raise ValueError(
            f"Low confidence ({confidence:.2f} < {confidence_threshold}); "
            f"answer '{winner}' is uncertain. Distribution: {dict(counts)}"
        )

    return {
        "answer": winner,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "vote_counts": dict(counts),
        "n_valid": len(valid),
    }


# --- Example usage ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    problem = (
        "A train travels 240 km in 3 hours. "
        "How long does it take to travel 400 km at the same speed?"
    )

    result = asyncio.run(
        self_consistent_answer(problem, n=7, temperature=0.7, confidence_threshold=0.5)
    )
    print(f"Answer: {result['answer']}")
    print(f"Confidence: {result['confidence']:.0%}  ({result['n_valid']} valid samples)")
    print(f"Vote distribution: {result['vote_counts']}")
A stripped-down synchronous version using the requests library, suitable for quick experimentation without async infrastructure:

# minimal_self_consistency.py — synchronous, for experimentation
import re
from collections import Counter
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

PROMPT = """Solve step by step. End with exactly: Final answer: <value>

Problem: {problem}
"""


def solve_with_sc(problem: str, n: int = 5) -> str:
    answers = []
    for _ in range(n):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            temperature=0.7,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT.format(problem=problem)}],
        )
        text = resp.content[0].text
        m = re.search(r"Final answer:\s*(.+)", text, re.IGNORECASE)
        if m:
            answers.append(m.group(1).strip().lower())

    if not answers:
        return "Could not determine answer"

    winner, count = Counter(answers).most_common(1)[0]
    print(f"Voted {count}/{n} for '{winner}'")
    return winner


# Quick test
ans = solve_with_sc("What is 17 × 24?")
print("Answer:", ans)  # Expected: 408
Example 2 — Agentic routing based on confidence
A realistic agent step that uses self-consistency to decide whether to proceed with a tool call or escalate to a stronger model:

# agentic_node.py — self-consistency as a guard in an agent pipeline
import asyncio
import re
from collections import Counter
from dataclasses import dataclass
from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic

client = AsyncAnthropic()

CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT = """You are a financial document classifier. Read the excerpt and classify it.

Categories: INVOICE, CONTRACT, RECEIPT, REPORT, OTHER

Reason through what you observe in the document, then write:
Final answer: <CATEGORY>

Excerpt:
{excerpt}
"""


@dataclass
class ClassificationResult:
    label: str
    confidence: float
    escalated: bool


async def classify_document(
    excerpt: str,
    n: int = 9,
    high_confidence: float = 0.7,
) -> ClassificationResult:
    """
    Classify with self-consistency. If confidence < high_confidence,
    escalate to a larger model rather than trusting the result.
    """
    tasks = []
    for _ in range(n):
        tasks.append(
            client.messages.create(
                model="claude-haiku-4-5",
                max_tokens=256,
                temperature=0.6,
                messages=[{
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT.format(excerpt=excerpt)
                }],
            )
        )
    responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

    labels = []
    for r in responses:
        if isinstance(r, Exception):
            continue  # skip failed samples
        text = r.content[0].text
        m = re.search(
            r"Final answer:\s*(INVOICE|CONTRACT|RECEIPT|REPORT|OTHER)",
            text, re.IGNORECASE
        )
        if m:
            labels.append(m.group(1).upper())

    if not labels:
        return ClassificationResult(label="OTHER", confidence=0.0, escalated=True)

    counts = Counter(labels)
    winner, top = counts.most_common(1)[0]
    confidence = top / len(labels)
    escalated = confidence < high_confidence

    if escalated:
        print(f"Low confidence ({confidence:.0%}); escalating to claude-opus-4-5")
        # Re-run with stronger model and n=1 (or higher n again)
        r = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            temperature=0.0,  # greedy — one definitive answer
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": CLASSIFICATION_PROMPT.format(excerpt=excerpt)
            }],
        )
        m = re.search(
            r"Final answer:\s*(INVOICE|CONTRACT|RECEIPT|REPORT|OTHER)",
            r.content[0].text, re.IGNORECASE
        )
        winner = m.group(1).upper() if m else "OTHER"

    return ClassificationResult(label=winner, confidence=confidence, escalated=escalated)


# --- Driver ---
async def main():
    excerpt = (
        "Invoice #INV-2024-0042\n"
        "Date: 2024-03-15\n"
        "Bill To: Acme Corp\n"
        "Total Due: $12,450.00\n"
        "Due Date: 2024-04-15"
    )
    result = await classify_document(excerpt)
    print(f"Label: {result.label}")
    print(f"Confidence: {result.confidence:.0%}")
    print(f"Escalated: {result.escalated}")

asyncio.run(main())

Consider a financial data extraction pipeline that processes thousands of earnings reports per day. Each report is a long PDF converted to text. The pipeline must extract a structured JSON object containing revenue, net income, and EPS for the current quarter. Errors in extraction flow into downstream financial models and trigger trading signals, so accuracy is critical.

A single-pass CoT prompt extracts the numbers correctly about 87 % of the time on a validation set. Self-consistency with N = 9 samples raises this to 94 %. The remaining 6 % produce a low vote margin (< 0.6) and are routed to a queue for human review, typically because the report uses non-standard formats or contains restated prior-quarter figures that confuse the model.

The pipeline architecture uses async Python with a semaphore to cap concurrency at 30 simultaneous API calls, staying within the API's rate limit. Each document dispatches 9 calls concurrently; wall-clock latency is barely higher than a single call. Total token cost increases 9×, but because the pipeline operates on documents that would otherwise require a human analyst, the cost is acceptable.
The most common failure: "408", "408.0", " 408 ", and "408 km" all represent the same answer but appear as four different candidates. The vote is split, and the wrong candidate may win.

Sampling at temperature 0 (greedy decoding) produces identical completions every time. All N samples will be the same, and majority vote degenerates to a single call. Always use T > 0 (typically 0.5 to 0.9) to generate diverse reasoning paths.

Attempting to vote over free-form summaries or creative outputs produces meaningless results. Every completion is unique; no answer accumulates more than one vote. Self-consistency requires a discrete, extractable answer space.

Discarding the confidence signal and always returning the majority vote, even when only 2 out of 9 samples agree, defeats the purpose. Set a threshold and act on low-confidence results explicitly — escalate, retry, or surface to a human.

When computing confidence, the denominator must be the number of successfully parsed samples, not the total N. If 3 out of 9 samples fail to produce a parseable answer and the remaining 6 split 4–2, the confidence is 4/6 = 0.67, not 4/9 = 0.44.
Calibrate N with an offline eval:
measure accuracy vs. N on a representative held-out set. For most tasks, gains plateau around N = 10 to 15. Do not default to N = 40 without evidence it helps.
instruct the model to always end with "Final answer: <value>". This makes extraction deterministic and robust across models and temperatures.
Use structured output for complex answers:
when the answer is a dict or list, use JSON mode or constrained generation to eliminate extraction ambiguity entirely.
or a thread pool to overlap API calls. Sequential sampling multiplies latency by N unnecessarily.
Respect rate limits with a semaphore:
cap simultaneous calls to stay within API tier limits; a semaphore of 20–50 concurrent calls is typical for production workloads.
store the vote counts alongside the final answer for debugging and post-hoc analysis. A 7:2:0 split tells a very different story than 9:0:0.
the right confidence threshold varies by task. A classification task with five labels should set a higher threshold than a binary yes/no question.
Test extraction on diverse model outputs:
the model may not always follow the delimiter instruction, especially at high temperatures. Build a fallback extractor that attempts regex on the last line when the delimiter is missing.

With identical prompt and output lengths, self-consistency costs exactly N× a single call. The dominant cost is output tokens (reasoning traces), not input tokens. A reasoning trace that is 600 tokens long with N = 9 costs 5 400 output tokens plus N × (input tokens). Switching from a large model to a smaller one for sampling and reserving the large model for escalation is a common strategy for controlling cost while preserving accuracy on hard cases.

With fully parallel dispatch, wall-clock latency equals the slowest of the N samples, not their sum. In practice, with async clients and no rate-limit contention, P99 latency of N = 9 samples is typically 1.3× to 2× a single sample — slower due to tail variance, but nowhere near 9×.

At high request volumes, N parallel sub-calls per request multiplies API concurrency N-fold. A service handling 100 requests per second with N = 9 generates 900 simultaneous API calls. This requires careful rate-limit budgeting and may require Batch API support (where available) to process large volumes cost-efficiently at lower priority.

An adaptive stopping rule can reduce average N: after each sample, check whether a majority threshold is already met with statistical significance. If after 4 samples, 4 out of 4 agree, stop early. This can reduce average N to 5–6 for easy inputs while still sampling 15+ times for genuinely hard inputs.
the primary interface for dispatching parallel samples. The async client supports concurrent requests natively via
abstraction can be wrapped in a list comprehension to gather N runs. The
method also supports batch prompts in one call.
and custom output parsers support retry logic; self-consistency can be layered on top of any retrieval-augmented generation pipeline.
grammar-constrained generation frameworks that enforce structured output eliminate the answer-extraction problem, making self-consistency simpler to implement correctly.
for offline workloads, batch all N samples into one API call to reduce cost and simplify orchestration.
module can be wrapped to run multiple samples and aggregate, and DSPy's optimiser can tune the underlying prompt for self-consistency scenarios.
Self-consistency is one of several techniques for improving LLM reasoning accuracy. The table below compares it to closely related approaches.
Sample N paths at T>0, majority vote
Decompose problem into sub-questions, solve in order
Explore a tree of reasoning steps with BFS/DFS
Train a model to score and select completions
Score each reasoning step, not just final answer
Model critiques and rewrites its own answer iteratively

Self-consistency sits at the sweet spot of low complexity, no training, and strong empirical gains for deterministic tasks. Tree of Thoughts offers more thorough search but at dramatically higher cost and complexity. Verifier-based methods exceed self-consistency accuracy but require a trained reward model.

If the prompt contains user-supplied content, an adversary may attempt to inject instructions that redirect all N samples to a particular answer. Because all samples share the same prompt, a successful injection corrupts the entire self-consistency run. Mitigations: sanitise user inputs before embedding them in the prompt; use a system-level "immune to instruction injection" framing; run self-consistency on extracted, structured sub-prompts rather than raw user text.

Self-consistency assumes errors across samples are approximately independent. When a question is systematically ambiguous or the model has a consistent bias for a topic, all N samples may converge on the same wrong answer. No voting strategy can correct this. Mitigations: diversify the prompt slightly across samples (different few-shot exemplar orderings, different instruction phrasings) to break correlation; monitor eval accuracy over time and alert when self-consistency no longer improves over single-pass.

With N parallel calls, any one transient API error will drop a sample. Implement per-sample exponential backoff and retry. Track the fraction of failed samples: if more than 30 % fail, abort and retry the entire self-consistency run rather than returning a vote over a tiny valid set with unreliable confidence.

A bug that sets N to a large number or that triggers self-consistency in a tight loop can exhaust token budgets quickly. Set a hard maximum on N (e.g. 20), implement per-request token budgets, and alert on anomalous spend. Use the Anthropic usage API to track real-time spend.
Regex extraction of the answer from model output is a surface for adversarial manipulation: the model (or injected content) may produce multiple "Final answer:" lines. Always extract the
occurrence of the delimiter, or use structured output (JSON mode) to eliminate ambiguity. Validate extracted values against an expected type or range before passing them to downstream tool calls.

Think of self-consistency as a committee of independent detectives solving the same case. Each detective follows their own line of reasoning, inspects different clues in the evidence, and writes an independent conclusion. When most of them converge on the same suspect, you can be reasonably confident. When the vote is split, you know the evidence is ambiguous and the case needs more investigation.
The committee analogy also clarifies the limits: if every detective received the same faulty briefing — a key witness lied to all of them — they will all be wrong together. Self-consistency improves
(systematic errors). A biased briefing corrupts the whole committee.

Self-consistency samples N independent chain-of-thought completions at non-zero temperature and selects the majority-vote answer, substantially improving accuracy over single-pass CoT on deterministic tasks.
The vote margin is a calibrated confidence signal: a low margin indicates genuine model uncertainty and should trigger escalation — more samples, a stronger model, or human review.
The technique requires no fine-tuning or external verifier, making it applicable to any model that supports temperature sampling.
Answer normalisation before voting is critical: without it, equivalent answers in different formats split the vote and the wrong candidate may win.
With fully concurrent dispatch, wall-clock latency is roughly 1.3–2× a single call rather than N×; total token cost is exactly N× and should be budgeted explicitly.
Self-consistency cannot fix systematic bias: if the model consistently misunderstands the question, all N samples will agree on the same wrong answer.
For offline or batch workloads, the Anthropic Message Batches API reduces cost by up to 50 % and simplifies orchestration by treating all N samples as one API call.
Adaptive stopping — halting early when a sample already exceeds a confidence threshold — reduces average N and cost while preserving full sample counts for genuinely hard inputs.
